subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear and logical structure with a defined introduction, body and conclusion
Coherent progression of sections (introduction â data collection â model testing â evaluation)
Effective use of headings, table of contents and appendices to guide the reader
weaknesses: Occasional digressions and verbosity (repeated definitions, raw tables in body) disrupt conciseness
Minor errors in captions and labels (e.g., âNASDAOâ typo) affect professionalism
Inconsistent bibliography formatting detracts from overall polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall the exploration is coherent and well structured, with a clear sequence and smooth transitions. However, repeated definitions, full raw tables in the main text and formatting inconsistencies prevent it from reaching the highest level of conciseness and polish, justifying a band 3.

strengths: Use of multiple representations (tables, scatterplots, overlaid regression curves) aids understanding
Key formulas (e.g. RÂ² = 1 â RSS/TSS) are correctly presented
Terminology and notation are generally understood
weaknesses: Frequent notation inconsistencies (mixing yÌ, v, Ã¢, missing subscripts) reduce clarity
Units of measurement (e.g., months since June 2014) are not always specified
Lack of residual or diagnostic plots limits depth of representation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the exploration employs effective representations and correct formulas, inconsistent notation, missing units and the absence of more sophisticated plots limit clarity and consistency, placing it at band 2.

strengths: Clear articulation of personal motivation in economics and financial markets
Studentâs choice of data range and model testing reflects individual initiative
Development of a percentage-error adjustment shows creative thinking
weaknesses: Reliance on standard Desmos outputs limits depth of independent methodology
Creative elements are modest and confined to basic model selection
Personal approach drives the topic but lacks deeper methodological innovation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates significant evidence of independent thinking and a clear personal approach, with some creative adaptation, meriting band 2 rather than the highest band of outstanding engagement.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on model limitations (overfitting, logistic plateau)
Basic critical analysis comparing RÂ² and percentage error
Clear link between findings and understanding of market behavior
weaknesses: Reflection lacks deeper statistical critique (e.g., confidence intervals, residual analysis)
Connections to broader mathematical subtleties are not fully explored
Suggestions for improvement are present but not elaborated with quantitative metrics
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation contains meaningful reflection and some critique, with a clear connection to learning outcomes. However, it stops short of substantial critical analysis and depth, aligning with band 2.

strengths: Relevant course-level mathematics (regression, RÂ², forecasting) applied appropriately
Equations and numerical computations are mostly correct
Justification of model choice shows sound understanding
weaknesses: Error-adjusted forecasting method is simplistic and lacks statistical rigor
Chronological ordering errors in data plotting may distort results
Major statistical tools (confidence intervals, cross-validation) are absent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses relevant HL syllabus mathematics correctly in parts, but the simplistic error adjustment, ordering mistakes and lack of robust statistical methods limit accuracy and depth, warranting band 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: Choosing data range, rejecting linear models, and developing an error-adjusted prediction show the studentâs own thinking beyond textbook procedure. However, most techniques are standard Desmos outputs, and interpretation remains basic. This constitutes âsignificantâ but not âoutstandingâ independent thinking.

Explanations of model choice, overfitting, logistic interpretation, and economic context demonstrate solid conceptual grasp, but occasional misconceptions (treating adjusted percentage error as structural improvement) show understanding is good rather than thorough or rigorous. Band 3 is therefore the best fit.

Equations derived in Desmos appear correct; RÂ² values and percentage-error computations are reasonable, though the âerror-adjustedâ prediction method is simplistic and may inflate accuracy claims. Overall mathematics is âmostly correct,â suiting band 3.

All material is broadly relevant, but the write-up is verbose: repeated definitions of RÂ², extensive quotations, full raw tables inside body, and an elaborate worked example of one percentage-error calculation. These unnecessary elements compromise conciseness, matching band 2 (âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ).

Notation for regression equations, RÂ², and percentage error is generally correct and applied throughÂ­out. Minor slips (occasionally mixing yÌ, v, or missing subscripts) prevent full consistency but do not impede understanding. This meets âmostly appropriateâ rather than flawless application.

Explanations are generally clear; terminology is used consistently in most sections. Occasional lapses (e.g., calling NASDAQ âNASDAOâ, mixing billions and index points) introduce minor inconsistency, so band 3 (âclear and mostly consistentâ) is justified.

Regression, RÂ², percentage error, and simple forecasting are all within the HL AI syllabus and clearly relevant. Mathematics is applied to answer the question but remains at standard course level without advanced depth; hence band 3 (relevant mathematics at course level used).

The student employs tables, scatterplots and overlaid regression curves alongside algebraic models and numerical summaries. These multiple representations are effective and well-integrated, though not sophisticated (e.g., no residual plots), therefore band 3 fits best.

The evaluation section discusses model limitations (overfitting, logistic plateau, single-variable focus) and suggests multiple regression as an extensionâevidence of meaningful reflection, though not deeply probing mathematical subtleties. Band 2 matches this level.

Applying three non-linear models and inventing a simple âpercentage-error adjustmentâ shows some initiative, yet the mathematics itself is conventional and the creative element modest. This aligns with âsignificant creativity and initiativeâ (band 2), not the sustained originality of band 3.

The study follows a clear sequenceâintroduction, data collection, model testing, comparison, prediction, conclusion, evaluationâand each part builds sensibly on the previous one. Transitions are mostly smooth, so the overall line of argument is easy to follow, although occasional digressions (e.g., lengthy definition sections) interrupt the flow. This aligns with âcoherent and clear logical development,â typical of band 3 rather than the consistently polished band 4.

There is basic critical analysis: comparison of RÂ² versus percentage error, comments on overfitting, recognition of model assumptions. The critique is relevant but not rigorous (e.g., no statistical tests, residual analysis), fitting âmeaningful analysis with some critiqueâ (band 2).

The student links findings to understanding of market behaviour and validates the hypothesis, showing a clearâthough not deeply extendedâconnection between reflection and learning. Band 2 (âclear connectionâ) is therefore appropriate.

Headings, table of contents, appendix and section labels make the structure obvious; introduction-method-results-evaluation is all present. While the flow is clear, there are minor ordering issues (results explained inside method, tables split over pages) so it does not reach the âexceptionally seamlessâ descriptor. Hence band 3 is most appropriate.

A personal economic interest is explained in the rationale, and the studentâs choices (e.g., why GDP real, why Daily Open for NASDAQ) shape the investigation. The approach is clearly personal but not deeply innovative, thus band 2.

annotations.comment: The studentâs personal interest in economics and financial markets is clearly articulated, demonstrating authentic personal engagement in the investigation.
The rationale provides context but would be strengthened by explicitly identifying a research gap or novel contribution of this study.
The hypothesis section uses <math display="inline">R^2</math> notation correctly but occasionally mixes variable notation. Ensure consistent use of LaTeX notation throughout.
The formula for <math display="block">R^2 = 1 - \tfrac{RSS}{TSS}</math> is correctly presented, reflecting sound mathematical communication.
The definitions of <math>\hat{v}</math> and <math>\overline{v}</math> introduce a different symbol than <math>y</math>. Unify the dependentâvariable notation to avoid confusion.
Figure 1 effectively visualizes GDP trend over time, but adding a residual plot would provide deeper insight into model fit.
Noting the inconsistent decimal places between GDP and S&P data is good, but discuss how this precision difference may affect regression coefficients.
Ordering GDP values numerically instead of chronologically can distort temporal relationships; maintain time sequence when plotting for regression.
When introducing the quadratic model, specify that <math>x</math> is measured in months since June 2014 so units are clear in the formula.
The logistic modelâs asymptote assumption may conflict with unlimited stock growth; critically reflect on its longâterm applicability in finance.
Table 1 caption contains a typo (âNASDAOâ); ensure accuracy in all headings and labels for professional presentation.
Demonstrating a singleâpoint percentage error is informative, but include error distribution (e.g., standard deviation) for a complete assessment.
The mean percentage error table shows quartic fitting slightly worse than quadratic. Discuss overfitting with quantitative metrics beyond mean error.
Adjusting predictions by adding average percentage error is not statistically rigorous. Consider constructing confidence intervals or using crossâvalidation instead.
The student rightly recognizes logistic plateau limitations; this evaluation is a strong critical reflection on model suitability.
The evaluation of model assumptions and external factors displays meaningful reflection on the investigationâs limitations.
Bibliography entries vary in format (italicization, punctuation). Adopt a single citation style (e.g., APA) for presentation coherence.
Use of average-error adjustment shows initiative but lacks statistical rigor; consider more robust forecasting methods.
The aim and approach justify data selection period well, showing thoughtful design in accounting for pandemic and recession effects.
The student effectively uses tables and regression graphs to compare multiple models, demonstrating clear mathematical communication.
When forecasting GDP for August 2025 using a linear trend, consider non-linear growth or seasonality instead of assuming a constant slope.
Excluding linear regression is appropriate given data complexity; this model selection shows good mathematical understanding.
Consistent notation for the dependent variable would enhance clarity (some analyses use <math>y</math>, others <math>v</math>).
The student justifies model choice and acknowledges overfitting risk in quartic regression, reflecting understanding of model complexity.
